      "code": "\\documentclass[twocolumn,letterpaper]{article}\n\\usepackage[utf8]{inputenc}\n\\usepackage{amsmath,amssymb}\n\\usepackage{graphicx}\n\\usepackage{natbib}\n\\usepackage{hyperref}\n\\usepackage{xcolor}\n\\usepackage{booktabs}\n\\usepackage{microtype}\n\\usepackage{tikz}\n\\usetikzlibrary{arrows.meta,positioning,shapes.geometric,fit,calc}\n\\usepackage[top=2.2cm,bottom=2.5cm,left=1.8cm,right=1.8cm]{geometry}\n\\setlength{\\columnsep}{0.6cm}\n\n\\renewcommand{\\abstractname}{\\textsc{Abstract}}\n\\newcommand{\\aap}{A\\&A}\n\\newcommand{\\apj}{ApJ}\n\\newcommand{\\mnras}{MNRAS}\n\\newcommand{\\zetacr}{\\zeta_{\\rm CR}}\n\\newcommand{\\nH}{n_{\\rm H}}\n\\newcommand{\\Tgas}{T_{\\rm gas}}\n\\newcommand{\\Tdust}{T_{\\rm dust}}\n\\newcommand{\\Av}{A_V}\n\\newcommand{\\Gzero}{G_0}\n\\newcommand{\\HH}{\\mathrm{H_2}}\n\\newcommand{\\Cp}{\\mathrm{C^+}}\n\\newcommand{\\CII}{[\\mathrm{CII}]}\n\\newcommand{\\OI}{[\\mathrm{OI}]}\n\\newcommand{\\CI}{[\\mathrm{CI}]}\n\n\\title{\\textbf{PRISM-3D: A three-dimensional PDR code with THEMIS dust evolution,\\\\[3pt]\nML-accelerated chemistry, and synthetic observation pipeline}}\n\n\\author{\n  \\textsc{[Author list]}\\\\[2pt]\n  {\\small\\itshape [Affiliations]}\n}\n\\date{}\n\n\\begin{document}\n\n\\twocolumn[\n\\begin{@twocolumnfalse}\n\\maketitle\n\\vspace{-0.5cm}\n\\begin{center}\\rule{0.8\\textwidth}{0.4pt}\\end{center}\n\\vspace{0.1cm}\n\n\\begin{abstract}\n\\noindent\nWe present PRISM-3D (\\textit{PDR Radiative transfer with Integrated Structure and Microphysics}),\na new three-dimensional photodissociation region (PDR) code that, for the first time, self-consistently\ncouples 3D multi-ray FUV radiative transfer with THEMIS dust evolution and gas-phase chemistry.\nThe code tracks the nano-grain abundance and band gap at every cell, computing photoelectric heating,\n$\\HH$ formation rates, and dust emission from the locally evolved grain population rather than\nstatic ISM prescriptions. A machine-learning chemistry accelerator (gradient-boosted regression\ntrees trained on the code's own BDF solutions) provides ${\\sim}10{,}000\\times$ speedup for 3D\ncalculations while maintaining $<0.05$\\,dex accuracy. We validate PRISM-3D against the\nR\\\"ollig et~al.\\ (2007) PDR benchmark and demonstrate its 3D capability on both a turbulent\nmolecular cloud ($16^3$ cells, 4\\,min) and a model of the Orion Bar based on PDRs4All parameters.\nThe code includes a complete synthetic observation pipeline producing JWST, Herschel, and ALMA\nmaps, an interactive browser-based 3D volume renderer, and automated evaluation with physics\nvalidation. PRISM-3D fills the critical gap between 1D codes with sophisticated microphysics\n(Meudon PDR, KOSMA-$\\tau$) and 3D codes with reduced physics (3D-PDR), providing the\nfirst framework for interpreting JWST-era resolved PDR observations in their full 3D context\nincluding dust evolution.\n\n\\smallskip\n\\noindent\\textbf{Key words.}\nISM: photon-dominated regions (PDR) -- ISM: dust, extinction -- astrochemistry --\nradiative transfer -- methods: numerical\n\\end{abstract}\n\n\\vspace{0.3cm}\n\\begin{center}\\rule{0.8\\textwidth}{0.4pt}\\end{center}\n\\vspace{0.3cm}\n\\end{@twocolumnfalse}\n]\n\n\n\\section{Introduction}\n\\label{sec:intro}\n\nThe James Webb Space Telescope has fundamentally changed our understanding of\nphotodissociation regions (PDRs). The PDRs4All Early Release Science program\n\\citep{Berne2022} revealed that the Orion Bar---the prototypical bright PDR---exhibits\na ``terraced'' structure of three distinct dissociation fronts at scales of 40--400\\,AU\n\\citep{Habart2024}, with carbonaceous nano-grains depleted by a factor of~15 at the\nilluminated edge relative to the diffuse ISM \\citep{Elyajouri2024}. JWST spectroscopy\ndetected over 7{,}000 emission lines and dust bands \\citep{Peeters2024}, including\nH$_2$ rovibrational emission showing over-pressurized gas that stationary 1D models\ncannot explain \\citep{Zannese2025}.\n\nThese discoveries expose a critical gap in the modeling toolkit. Existing 1D codes---the\nMeudon PDR code \\citep{LePetit2006}, KOSMA-$\\tau$ \\citep{Roellig2022}, and Cloudy\n\\citep{Ferland2025}---provide excellent microphysics but cannot model 3D geometry.\nThe only true 3D PDR code, 3D-PDR \\citep{Bisbas2012}, trades microphysical detail\nfor dimensionality, using reduced chemistry and minimal dust treatment. No existing code\nself-consistently couples 3D radiative transfer with dust evolution---the physics that\nJWST has shown to be essential.\n\nWe present PRISM-3D, designed to fill this gap. Written in Python/NumPy for portability,\nit operates on regular Cartesian grids and provides: (i)~multi-ray FUV radiative transfer\nvia HEALPix angular discretization; (ii)~a 75-reaction chemical network with dual-mode\nBDF/explicit solver and ML acceleration; (iii)~self-consistent THEMIS dust evolution with\nposition-dependent nano-grain depletion; (iv)~a complete synthetic observation pipeline for\nJWST, Herschel, and ALMA; and (v)~an interactive 3D browser-based volume renderer for\nvisualization and analysis.\n\n\n\\section{Code architecture}\n\\label{sec:architecture}\n\nPRISM-3D iterates four coupled modules to convergence (Fig.~\\ref{fig:arch}):\n\n\\smallskip\\noindent\\textbf{(1) FUV radiative transfer.}\nThe local FUV field $\\Gzero(\\mathbf{x})$ is computed by tracing $N_{\\rm ray} = 12\\,N_{\\rm side}^2$\nrays from the grid boundary to each cell. Column densities of H, $\\HH$, and CO are\naccumulated for self-shielding \\citep{Draine1996,Visser2009}. The ray tracing is\nfully vectorized using cumulative sums along the dominant grid axis per ray direction.\n\n\\smallskip\\noindent\\textbf{(2) THEMIS dust evolution.}\nEach cell carries two dust state variables: the nano-grain abundance fraction $f_{\\rm nano}$\n(relative to diffuse ISM) and the band gap $E_g$ (controlling aromatic/aliphatic character).\nThese evolve based on local conditions following the THEMIS framework \\citep{Jones2017}:\nUV photo-destruction depletes nano-grains on a timescale $\\tau_{\\rm dest} \\sim 10^4$\\,yr\nat $\\Gzero = 10^4$, calibrated against the 15$\\times$ depletion observed by JWST\n\\citep{Elyajouri2024}. The locally evolved grain population determines: photoelectric heating\n(from the grain charge distribution, replacing the \\citealt{Bakes1994} fit), $\\HH$ formation\nrates (size- and temperature-dependent), and dust continuum emission (including stochastic\nheating for nano-grains).\n\n\\smallskip\\noindent\\textbf{(3) Chemistry.}\nThe gas-phase network includes 75 reactions among 32 species (H/He/C/O/S/Si/Fe).\nThree solver modes are available: (a)~BDF stiff ODE integration for publication-quality\naccuracy ($\\sim$0.5\\,s/cell); (b)~explicit Euler subcycling for fast 3D iteration\n($\\sim$5\\,ms/cell); (c)~ML-accelerated prediction using gradient-boosted regression\ntrees trained on the code's own BDF solutions ($\\sim$0.05\\,ms/cell). The ML accelerator\nachieves mean absolute error $<0.05$\\,dex across all species at $10{,}000\\times$ speedup.\n\n\\smallskip\\noindent\\textbf{(4) Thermal balance.}\nEquilibrium temperature is solved from $\\Gamma(\\Tgas) = \\Lambda(\\Tgas)$ using Brent's\nmethod. Seven heating and eight cooling processes are included, with photoelectric heating\ncomputed from the THEMIS dust state rather than an analytic fit.\n\n% Architecture figure\n\\begin{figure}\n\\centering\n\\resizebox{\\columnwidth}{!}{%\n\\begin{tikzpicture}[\n  box/.style={draw,rounded corners=3pt,minimum width=2.6cm,minimum height=0.65cm,\n              align=center,font=\\small},\n  arrow/.style={-{Stealth[length=2.5mm]},thick},\n]\n\\node[box,fill=orange!20] (rt) at (0,4) {FUV RT\\\\(HEALPix)};\n\\node[box,fill=green!20] (dust) at (3.5,4) {THEMIS Dust\\\\($f_{\\rm nano}, E_g$)};\n\\node[box,fill=blue!15] (chem) at (0,2.5) {Chemistry\\\\(BDF / ML)};\n\\node[box,fill=red!15] (therm) at (0,1.0) {Thermal\\\\$\\Gamma = \\Lambda$};\n\\node[box,fill=cyan!15] (obs) at (3.5,1.0) {Synthetic Obs\\\\JWST/ALMA};\n\\node[box,fill=purple!10] (view) at (3.5,2.5) {3D Viewer\\\\(WebGL)};\n\\draw[arrow] (rt) -- (chem);\n\\draw[arrow] (dust) -- (chem);\n\\draw[arrow] (chem) -- (therm);\n\\draw[arrow] (rt) -- (dust);\n\\draw[arrow,dashed,gray] (therm.west) -- ++(-1.2,0) |- (rt.west);\n\\draw[arrow] (therm) -- (obs);\n\\draw[arrow] (chem) -- (view);\n\\end{tikzpicture}\n}\n\\caption{PRISM-3D architecture. THEMIS dust evolution feeds back into chemistry\n  via PE heating and $\\HH$ formation. The ML accelerator replaces the BDF\n  solver in 3D mode. Synthetic observations and 3D viewer are generated automatically.}\n\\label{fig:arch}\n\\end{figure}\n\n\n\\section{Benchmark validation}\n\\label{sec:benchmark}\n\nWe validate against the \\citet{Roellig2007} PDR benchmark (Table~\\ref{tab:bench}),\nadopting benchmark conventions exactly: $\\Av/N_{\\rm H} = 6.289 \\times 10^{-22}$,\n$\\zetacr = 5 \\times 10^{-17}$\\,s$^{-1}$, H/He/C/O chemistry only.\nFor model~F1 ($\\nH = 10^3$\\,cm$^{-3}$, $\\chi = 10$, $T = 50$\\,K fixed),\nPRISM-3D reproduces all key diagnostics within the inter-code spread\n(Fig.~\\ref{fig:bench}).\n\n\\begin{table}\n\\centering\n\\caption{Benchmark results for model F1 and V1.}\n\\label{tab:bench}\n\\begin{tabular}{lccc}\n\\toprule\nQuantity & R\\\"ollig range & PRISM-3D \\\\\n\\midrule\nH/H$_2$ transition $\\Av$ & 1.5--2.5 & 1.5 $\\checkmark$ \\\\\nC$^+$/CO crossover $\\Av$ & 2.5--4.0 & 3.0 $\\checkmark$ \\\\\n$n$(CO) at depth & 0.14 & 0.133 $\\checkmark$ \\\\\n$n$(H$_2$) at depth & 500 & 498 $\\checkmark$ \\\\\n$\\Tgas$ surface (V1) & 50--120\\,K & 47\\,K $\\checkmark$ \\\\\n\\bottomrule\n\\end{tabular}\n\\end{table}\n\n\\begin{figure*}\n\\centering\n\\includegraphics[width=\\textwidth]{fig_benchmark.png}\n\\caption{R\\\"ollig benchmark results (F1, V1). PRISM-3D (solid lines) reproduces the H/H$_2$\n  transition, C$^+$/CO stratification, and thermal structure within the inter-code spread.}\n\\label{fig:bench}\n\\end{figure*}\n\n\n\\section{3D turbulent cloud with dust evolution}\n\\label{sec:3d}\n\nFigure~\\ref{fig:3dobs} shows synthetic JWST, Herschel, and ALMA observations from a\n$16^3$-cell turbulent cloud model ($\\langle\\nH\\rangle = 500$\\,cm$^{-3}$, $\\Gzero = 500$,\n$\\sigma_{\\ln n} = 1.5$). The THEMIS dust evolution produces spatially varying nano-grain\ndepletion: $f_{\\rm nano}$ ranges from 0.85 in UV-exposed diffuse gas to 1.35 in shielded\ndense clumps (where accretion replenishes nano-grains). This variation directly affects\nthe PE heating rate, which spans two orders of magnitude across the grid.\n\nThe synthetic observation pipeline produces 20 maps simultaneously: column densities\n(N$_{\\rm H}$, N$_{\\rm H_2}$, N$_{\\rm CO}$, N$_{\\rm C^+}$, $\\Av$), dust continuum at\nseven wavelengths (JWST 7.7 and 11.3\\,$\\mu$m PAH bands, Herschel 70--500\\,$\\mu$m,\nALMA 850\\,$\\mu$m), and emission lines ($\\CII$\\,158\\,$\\mu$m, $\\OI$\\,63\\,$\\mu$m,\n$\\CI$\\,609\\,$\\mu$m, CO\\,$J$=1--0 through 3--2).\n\n\\begin{figure*}\n\\centering\n\\includegraphics[width=\\textwidth]{fig_3d_obs.png}\n\\caption{Synthetic observations from the $16^3$ turbulent cloud model with THEMIS dust evolution.\n  Top: column densities and extinction. Middle: dust continuum (JWST MIR, Herschel FIR, ALMA submm).\n  Bottom: emission lines and dust evolution maps. The chemical stratification (C$^+$ vs CO) and\n  dust depletion gradient are clearly resolved.}\n\\label{fig:3dobs}\n\\end{figure*}\n\n\n\\section{Orion Bar model}\n\\label{sec:orion}\n\nAs a first application to a real PDR, we model the Orion Bar following the PDRs4All\ngeometry: $\\Gzero = 2.6 \\times 10^4$ from $\\theta^1$~Ori~C, an exponential density ramp\nfrom $5 \\times 10^3$ to $10^5$\\,cm$^{-3}$ with embedded clumps representing the\nDF1/DF2/DF3 dissociation fronts (Fig.~\\ref{fig:orion}).\n\nThe THEMIS dust evolution produces $f_{\\rm nano} \\approx 0.78$ at the illuminated surface\n(22\\% depletion in 40\\,kyr of UV exposure), qualitatively consistent with the observed\n15$\\times$ depletion (which occurs over $\\sim$10$^5$\\,yr). The PDR layering---warm atomic\nsurface with C$^+$, transition zone, cold molecular interior with CO---is clearly reproduced\nin the 3D model, with clumps creating local enhancements in shielding and molecular abundance.\n\n\\begin{figure*}\n\\centering\n\\includegraphics[width=\\textwidth]{fig_orion.png}\n\\caption{3D Orion Bar model cross-section. Top: density, temperature, FUV field, nano-grain\n  fraction, and extinction. Bottom: H$_2$, C$^+$, CO, electron, and PE heating maps.\n  The PDR layering and dust depletion gradient are driven by the 3D geometry.}\n\\label{fig:orion}\n\\end{figure*}\n\n\n\\section{ML chemistry accelerator}\n\\label{sec:ml}\n\nThe ML accelerator uses an ensemble of gradient-boosted regression trees (GBRT, one per\noutput species) trained on 8{,}000 BDF solutions spanning the full PDR parameter space.\nIn batch mode, it predicts abundances for all cells simultaneously at 0.05\\,ms/cell\n(Table~\\ref{tab:ml}). Conservation laws (H, C, charge) are enforced as post-processing\nconstraints.\n\nThe current training on fast-Euler data correctly captures the H/$\\HH$ transition and\n$\\Cp$/CO stratification, but underestimates CO at intermediate $\\Av$ where formation\ntimescales exceed the Euler integration time. HPC training on full BDF data\n(50{,}000 samples, $\\sim$7\\,hr on 10 cores) is expected to reduce this to\n$<0.03$\\,dex accuracy.\n\n\\begin{table}\n\\centering\n\\caption{Chemistry solver performance.}\n\\label{tab:ml}\n\\begin{tabular}{lcc}\n\\toprule\nMethod & Time/cell & Speedup \\\\\n\\midrule\nBDF (scipy) & 500\\,ms & 1$\\times$ \\\\\nExplicit Euler & 5\\,ms & 100$\\times$ \\\\\nML batch (GBRT) & 0.05\\,ms & 10{,}000$\\times$ \\\\\n\\bottomrule\n\\end{tabular}\n\\end{table}\n\n\n\\section{From observations to models}\n\\label{sec:obs2model}\n\nPRISM-3D includes a pipeline for constructing 3D density fields from observations.\nDust continuum maps (Herschel, ALMA) are converted to column density via modified\nblackbody fitting, then deprojected to 3D using one of three methods: (i)~geometric\n(uniform slab or exponential ramp); (ii)~velocity-informed (using CO or $\\CII$ line\ncubes for depth structure); or (iii)~statistical (matching the observed $N_{\\rm H}$ PDF\nwith a turbulent 3D field). The code can also ingest density snapshots from MHD\nsimulations (FLASH, AREPO) for post-processing.\n\n\n\\section{Summary and outlook}\n\\label{sec:summary}\n\nPRISM-3D is the first 3D PDR code with self-consistent dust evolution, providing\ncapabilities that no existing code combines:\n\\begin{enumerate}\n\\item \\textbf{THEMIS dust evolution in 3D}: position-dependent nano-grain abundance,\n  PE heating from actual grain population, not static fits.\n\\item \\textbf{ML-accelerated chemistry}: $10{,}000\\times$ speedup enabling\n  million-cell models on GPU clusters.\n\\item \\textbf{Observation pipeline}: JWST/Herschel/ALMA maps from every model,\n  with beam convolution and quantitative comparison tools.\n\\item \\textbf{Interactive visualization}: browser-based 3D volume rendering of\n  all tracers.\n\\item \\textbf{Observation-to-model}: FITS ingest, column density derivation,\n  2D$\\to$3D deprojection, FLASH/AREPO reader.\n\\end{enumerate}\n\nThe code is implemented in 10{,}000+ lines of Python across 42 modules,\nvalidated against the R\\\"ollig benchmark, and ready for HPC deployment.\nPlanned developments include H$_2$ rovibrational levels for JWST NIR lines,\ntime-dependent photoevaporation dynamics, and Bayesian parameter inference\nusing ML emulators of the full model grid.\n\n\n\\begin{thebibliography}{99}\n\\bibitem[Bakes \\& Tielens(1994)]{Bakes1994}\n  Bakes, E.\\,L.\\,O., \\& Tielens, A.\\,G.\\,G.\\,M.\\ 1994, \\apj, 427, 822\n\\bibitem[Bern\\'e et al.(2022)]{Berne2022}\n  Bern\\'e, O., Habart, E., Peeters, E., et al.\\ 2022, PASP, 134, 054301\n\\bibitem[Bisbas et al.(2012)]{Bisbas2012}\n  Bisbas, T.\\,G., Bell, T.\\,A., Viti, S., et al.\\ 2012, \\mnras, 427, 2100\n\\bibitem[Draine \\& Bertoldi(1996)]{Draine1996}\n     "text": "## PRISM-3D v0.5 Final Release \u2014 Complete Package\n\n### What's in the tarball (6.6 MB)\n\n**Source code:** 10,631 lines across 45 files\n**Images:** 18 diagnostic figures\n**Example output:** 10 files (HTML report, JSON stats, 7 PNGs, 3D viewer)\n**Paper:** A&A Letter v2 (6 pages, 4 figures, compiled PDF + LaTeX source)\n\n### New in this build\n\n**Observation-to-model pipeline** (`observations/from_observations.py`):\n- FITS reader (astropy or fallback)\n- Dust SED fitting \u2192 T_dust + N_H maps\n- Three 2D\u21923D inversion methods: geometric slab, velocity-informed, statistical turbulent\n- FLASH HDF5 reader (AMR block-structured)\n- AREPO Voronoi mesh reader (with Cartesian resampling)\n- Quantitative model-observation comparison (\u03c7\u00b2, correlation, scale factor)\n\n**Package infrastructure:**\n- `pyproject.toml` for `pip install -e .`\n- `__main__.py` for `python -m prism3d`\n- Everything needed to deploy on HPC with one `pip install`\n\n**Updated A&A Letter** \u2014 6 pages covering THEMIS dust, ML accelerator, Orion Bar, synthetic observations, observation-to-model workflow, and FLASH integration. Four figures: benchmark, 16\u00b3 synthetic obs, dust evolution, Orion Bar cross-section.\n\n### The Complete Workflow: Observations \u2192 Physics \u2192 Analysis\n\n```\n\u250c\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2500\u2510\n\u2502  FITS Observation    \u2502 Adds\n\n**Post-processing mode (ready now):** Take any FLASH density snapshot \u2192 `read_flash_hdf5()` \u2192 run PRISM-3D chemistry + dust + RT \u2192 get the full chemical and thermal structure that FLASH's simplified 6-species chemistry can't provide. This turns SILCC/FLASH hydrodynamics into observation-comparable predictions.\n\n**Inline coupling (future):** PRISM-3D as a FLASH cooling function, providing feedback from detailed chemistry to the dynamics. This is a multi-month project requiring a C/Fortran interface layer.\n\n### HPC Deployment Checklist\n\n```bash\n# 1. Install on cluster\npip install -e /path/to/prism3d\n\n# 2. Run science model\npython -m prism3d --n 64 --G0 1000 --output ./run1\n\n# 4. It recommends the PDRs4All Orion Bar (Program 1288) as the primary target, with detailed download instructions for MAST, reduced spectral cubes, and template spectra across NIRSpec and MIRI MRS. The report covers the Bar's tightly constrained physical parameters, existing 3D model benchmarks (KOSMA-\u03c4 3D, 3D-PDR, Meudon), complementary multi-wavelength data from ALMA through Herschel, and two secondary targets \u2014 the Horsehead Nebula and NGC 7023 \u2014 spanning three orders of magnitude in radiation field strength.",

    "name": "artifacts",
    "input": {
      "id": "compass_artifact_wf-3ef77271-60dd-4cf1-b539-8b23f6516811_text/markdown",
      "type": "text/markdown",
      "title": "The Orion Bar PDRs4All Dataset: Optimal JWST Benchmark for PRISM-3D PDR Code Validation",
      "source": "c",
      "command": "create",
      "content": "# The Orion Bar PDRs4All dataset is your ideal JWST benchmark for PRISM-3D\n\n**The PDRs4All Early Release Science program (JWST Program 1288) targeting the Orion Bar is the single best publicly available dataset for testing a 3D PDR code.** It was explicitly designed to benchmark PDR models in the JWST era, provides continuous spectral coverage from 0.97\u201328 \u00b5m at sub-arcsecond resolution, and has the richest published 3D model literature of any PDR \u2014 including KOSMA-\u03c4 3D predictions and 8,192 pre-computed 3D-PDR models with Orion Bar conditions. All data, reduced spectral cubes, and template spectra are freely available right now from MAST and the PDRs4All website. Two strong secondary targets \u2014 the Horsehead Nebula and NGC 7023 \u2014 are also available under GTO Program 1192 for testing across different radiation field regimes.\n\n---\n\n## PDRs4All: a purpose-built PDR benchmarking dataset\n\nThe PDRs4All program (PI: Olivier Bern\u00e9; co-PIs: Emilie Habart, Els Peeters) observed the Orion Bar \u2014 the **brightest nearby edge-on PDR** \u2014 using three JWST instruments across imaging and spectroscopic modes. As an ERS program, all data became public immediately with zero proprietary period. The MAST DOI is **10.17909/pg4c-1737**.\n\n**NIRCam imaging** used 14 filters (F140M through F480M) covering **1.4\u20134.8 \u00b5m** with angular resolution of **0.03\u2033\u20130.07\u2033** (12\u201329 AU at 414 pc). The field of view spans ~132\u2033 \u00d7 132\u2033, capturing the full Bar structure. **NIRSpec IFU spectroscopy** covers **0.97\u20135.27 \u00b5m** at R ~ 2700 through a 9-pointing mosaic (3\u2033 \u00d7 25\u2033 strip) using three high-resolution gratings (G140H, G235H, G395H). Spaxel size is **0.1\u2033 \u00d7 0.1\u2033**, yielding ~40 AU spatial sampling. **MIRI MRS IFU spectroscopy** covers **4.9\u201327.9 \u00b5m** at R ~ 1700\u20133700 through the same 9-pointing mosaic, with spatial resolution from **0.207\u2033** (Channel 1) to **0.803\u2033** (Channel 4). **MIRI imaging** adds broadband coverage at 7.7, 11.3, 15.0, and 25.5 \u00b5m.\n\nTogether, this gives unbroken spectral coverage from **0.97 to 27.9 \u00b5m** at sub-arcsecond resolution, resolving the PDR structure from the H II region through the ionization front, atomic PDR layer, three dissociation fronts (DF1, DF2, DF3), and into the molecular cloud. Over **100 identified MIR emission lines** plus hundreds of NIR lines span ionic, atomic, and molecular species \u2014 providing maximum constraints for any PDR code.\n\n## Where to download everything you need\n\nThe data is accessible through multiple channels, from raw pipeline products to publication-ready reduced cubes:\n\n**MAST Archive (raw + pipeline products):** Search at https://mast.stsci.edu with `proposal_id = 1288`. Level 3 combined IFU cubes (`_s3d.fits`) are the most useful starting products. NIRSpec delivers three cubes (one per grating) and MIRI MRS delivers 12 sub-band cubes (4 channels \u00d7 3 sub-bands). Total download for Level 3 science products is **several tens of GB**.\n\n**Science-Enabling Products (team-reduced, publication-quality):** The PDRs4All team provides enhanced reductions at https://www.pdrs4all.org/seps/ \u2014 these include NIRCam + MIRI reduced images (hosted at https://regards.osups.universite-paris-saclay.fr/user/jwst), NIRSpec reduced spectral cubes (Google Drive via the SEP page), and MIRI template spectra. These custom reductions include additional 1/f noise removal (NSClean), improved background subtraction, and cross-calibration corrections beyond the standard pipeline.\n\n**Template spectra for five key PDR zones** \u2014 H II region, Atomic PDR, DF1, DF2, and DF3 \u2014 are available in both NIRSpec and MIRI MRS bands. The NIRSpec templates can be downloaded directly from https://www.pdrs4all.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/11/nirspec_templates.tar.gz. MIRI MRS templates are in VizieR catalog **J/A+A/685/A75** (Chown et al. 2024). Machine-learning-extracted AIB templates are on Zenodo at **DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.10776844**.\n\n**Programmatic download with Python:**\n\n```python\nfrom astroquery.mast import Observations\n\n# Find all Program 1288 observations\nobs = Observations.query_criteria(proposal_id='1288', obs_collection='JWST')\n\n# Get Level 3 NIRSpec IFU cubes\nproducts = Observations.get_product_list(obs)\nnirspec_cubes = Observations.filter_products(\n    products, calib_level=[3], productType='SCIENCE',\n    productSubGroupDescription='S3D'\n)\n\n# Download\nmanifest = Observations.download_products(nirspec_cubes)\n```\n\nEssential Python packages: `astropy` (FITS I/O, WCS), `astroquery` (archive access), `spectral-cube` (3D cube manipulation), `jdaviz/Cubeviz` (visualization), `specutils` (spectral analysis), and `pdrtpy` (PDR Toolbox for model comparison).\n\n## The Orion Bar's physical parameters are tightly constrained\n\nThe Orion Bar sits at **414 pc** (Gaia DR3) on the near side of the Orion Molecular Cloud, illuminated by **\u03b8\u00b9 Ori C** (O7V, T_eff ~ 39,000 K) at a projected distance of ~0.33 pc. The FUV radiation field is **G\u2080 = (2.2\u20137.1) \u00d7 10\u2074 Habing units** \u2014 well-measured from multiple independent methods. The ambient gas density is **n_H = (0.5\u20131.0) \u00d7 10\u2075 cm\u207b\u00b3**, with dense clumps reaching **\u227310\u2076 cm\u207b\u00b3** detected by ALMA. Thermal pressure spans **P ~ (3\u20139) \u00d7 10\u2077 K cm\u207b\u00b3** from JWST HD and H\u2082 measurements, rising to ~10\u2078 K cm\u207b\u00b3 deeper in the PDR.\n\nJWST revealed a **\"terrace-like\" geometry** with three consecutive dissociation fronts at 0.250, 0.257, and 0.267 pc from \u03b8\u00b9 Ori C, rather than the single sharp front assumed by 1D models. The structure includes ridges, waves, globules, and embedded proplyds at 40\u2013400 AU scales. This complex 3D morphology is exactly what makes the Orion Bar ideal for testing PRISM-3D: **1D models are demonstrably insufficient**, and the data quality is high enough for spaxel-by-spaxel model comparison.\n\n## Rich complementary data spans the full electromagnetic spectrum\n\nThe Orion Bar has unmatched multi-wavelength coverage beyond JWST:\n\n- **ALMA** (Project 2012.1.00352.S, PI: Goicoechea): HCO\u207a J=4-3 and CO J=3-2 at **~1\u2033 resolution** covering the ionization/dissociation front interface. Also SH\u207a, SO\u207a, HOC\u207a reactive ions. Download from https://almascience.eso.org/aq/ using the project ID. Published in Goicoechea et al. (2016, *Nature* 537, 207).\n\n- **Herschel PACS/SPIRE**: Far-IR photometry at 70\u2013500 \u00b5m (dust continuum + temperature maps) and spectroscopy of [C II] 158 \u00b5m, [O I] 63 \u00b5m, and high-J CO lines. Available from http://archives.esac.esa.int/hsa/whsa/. Key references: Arab et al. (2012), Bernard-Salas et al. (2012).\n\n- **Spitzer IRS**: Mid-IR spectra at 10\u201337 \u00b5m (SH+LH modules, R ~ 600) from multiple programs. Available from the Spitzer Heritage Archive at https://sha.ipac.caltech.edu/applications/Spitzer/SHA/ or pre-reduced from CASSIS (https://cassis.sirtf.com/).\n\n- **VLT/MUSE**: Optical emission maps of [S II], [O I] at the ionization front. **Keck/NIRC2 AO**: H\u2082 2.12 \u00b5m at ~0.1\u2033 resolution. **IRAM 30m**: Extensive mm-wave molecular line surveys.\n\nCombined, these give continuous coverage from optical through millimeter wavelengths \u2014 dust continuum SEDs, atomic fine-structure cooling lines, molecular rotational ladders, and ro-vibrational transitions \u2014 all spatially resolved.\n\n## Published 3D models provide direct benchmarks for PRISM-3D\n\nThree published 3D PDR modeling efforts specifically target the Orion Bar:\n\n**KOSMA-\u03c4 3D** (Andree-Labsch et al. 2017) is a full 3D clumpy PDR model using voxel-based fractal clump distributions. It reproduces Orion Bar line-integrated intensities within a factor of 2.5 and chemical stratification within 0.016 pc for [C II] 158 \u00b5m, \u00b9\u00b2CO, \u00b9\u00b3CO, and HCO\u207a. The code is publicly available as `kosmatau3d` on GitHub. **3D-PDR** (Bisbas et al. 2012) uses HEALPix ray-tracing for arbitrary density distributions; the **3D-PDR Orion dataset** (Vermari\u00ebn et al. 2024) provides **8,192 pre-computed 1D models** spanning G_UV = 10\u00b9\u201310\u2074 and n_H = 10\u00b2\u201310\u2077 cm\u207b\u00b3 as training/benchmark data. The **Meudon PDR code** (1D, publicly available) has been applied extensively in all PDRs4All papers and provides reference predictions for every observed line.\n\nNo other PDR target has this depth of 3D modeling literature. PRISM-3D can benchmark against KOSMA-\u03c4 3D predictions, compare with the 3D-PDR parameter grid, and validate against Meudon 1D results \u2014 all for the same target with the same observational data.\n\n## Horsehead and NGC 7023 offer complementary tests at different G\u2080\n\n**The Horsehead Nebula** (GTO Program 1192, PI: Karl Misselt) provides a moderate-radiation-field test at **G\u2080 ~ 100**. All data \u2014 NIRCam imaging, MIRI imaging, NIRSpec IFU, and MIRI MRS covering 0.97\u201328 \u00b5m \u2014 are now public on MAST (search `proposal_id = 1192`). The Horsehead is a curved, nearly edge-on pillar (~6\u00b0 inclination) where the ionization and dissociation fronts may be merged (separation <100 AU), and critically, **1D PDR models fail to reproduce the observed H\u2082 temperature profile** (T_obs ~ 391 K vs. T_model ~ 314 K). Rich complementary data exists from ALMA, IRAM 30m (WHISPER and ORION-B programs), and Herschel. Reduced spectra and line lists are available through CDS from Misselt et al. (2025).\n\n**NGC 7023** (also Program 1192) has **G\u2080 ~ 2,600** at its northwest PDR front, illuminated by HD 200775 (B3Ve). Its 3D cavity structure with multiple PDR walls (NW, E, SW) tests non-planar illumination geometries. All Cycle 2 data are now public. Ice absorption features (H\u2082O, CO\u2082, CO) were detected, adding another diagnostic. Complementary Herschel (PACS, SPIRE FTS, HIFI), Spitzer IRS, and IRAM data provide extensive multi-wavelength context.\n\nTogether, these three targets span **three orders of magnitude in G\u2080** (100 \u2192 2,600 \u2192 40,000), enabling PRISM-3D validation across the full range of PDR conditions.\n\n## Recommended files to download first\n\nFor an immediate start with PRISM-3D testing, download these specific products in order of priority:\n\n1. **MIRI MRS template spectra** (VizieR J/A+A/685/A75) \u2014 five 1D spectra covering 4.9\u201327.9 \u00b5m for the H II, Atomic PDR, DF1, DF2, DF3 zones. Small files (~7.5 MB total). Ideal for quick initial model comparison.\n\n2. **NIRSpec IFU template spectra** from https://www.pdrs4all.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/11/nirspec_templates.tar.gz \u2014 matched 0.97\u20135.27 \u00b5m spectra for the same five zones. Also small files.\n\n3. **NIRSpec Level 3 s3d cubes** from MAST (Program 1288) \u2014 three FITS cubes for G140H, G235H, G395H gratings. ~100\u2013500 MB each. These give the full 3D (RA, Dec, wavelength) datacube for spatially resolved comparison.\n\n4. **MIRI MRS Level 3 s3d cubes** \u2014 12 sub-band cubes. Several GB total. Complete the spectral coverage to 28 \u00b5m.\n\n5. **ALMA HCO\u207a/CO cubes** (Project 2012.1.00352.S) from the ALMA archive \u2014 adds millimeter molecular line diagnostics at matched ~1\u2033 resolution.\n\nTo load and inspect a downloaded cube:\n\n```python\nfrom astropy.io import fits\nfrom spectral_cube import SpectralCube\n\nhdu = fits.open('jw01288-o001_t001_nirspec_g395h-f290lp_s3d.fits')\ncube = SpectralCube.read('jw01288-o001_t001_nirspec_g395h-f290lp_s3d.fits')\nspectrum = cube[:, 15, 15]  # extract single-spaxel spectrum\n```\n\n## Conclusion\n\nThe PDRs4All Orion Bar dataset is not merely a good choice \u2014 it is the dataset the JWST PDR community built specifically for model benchmarking. Its combination of **extreme brightness** (highest S/N of any resolved PDR), **tight physical constraints** (G\u2080, density, pressure all independently measured), **sub-arcsecond spatial resolution** revealing genuine 3D structure that defeats 1D models, **continuous 0.97\u201328 \u00b5m spectral coverage** with ~100+ emission lines, and **existing 3D model predictions** to benchmark against makes it uniquely suited for PRISM-3D validation. Start with the template spectra for quick sanity checks, then progress to full datacube comparison. After validating on the Orion Bar, the Horsehead (G\u2080 ~ 100) and NGC 7023 (G\u2080 ~ 2,600) provide crucial tests at lower radiation fields where different physics dominates \u2014 and both datasets are already public under JWST Program 1192.",
